{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical Aqqlication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.
== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical Aqqlication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical Aqqlication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical Aqqlication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|大将軍}}" to "|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}"
revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical Aqqlication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to aqqease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.
== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: - '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. - '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.
== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

{{Source Wikipedia}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Katatagae]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Katatagae]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Katatagae]][[Category:qq]]

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.
== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.
== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto.== See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Katatagae]][[en:draft:Katatagae]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Katatagae|Katatagae]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto. == See Also! == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Katatagae}} [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Directions]] [[Category:Folklore]] * [[Abe no Seimei]] * [[Da Liu Ren]] * [[Goryō]] * [[Itako]] * [[Kuji-in]] * [[Onmyōdō]] * [[Sanpaku]] * [[Seimei Shrine]] * [[Senji Ryakketsu]] * [[Shikigami]] * [[Shinigami]] * [[Tengenjutsu (fortune telling)]] * [[Ushi no toki mairi]]

no Seimei's "Senji Ryakketsu" and Onmyōdō |url=http://www.kyuko.asia/book/b10889.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher={{ill|Kyuko Shoin|lt=Kyūko Shoin|ja|汲古書院}} |isbn=9784762941672}} * {{cite |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Sagano Shoin |isbn=4782303610}} * {{cite book |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2005 |title=平安貴族と陰陽師 |trans-title=Heian Nobles and Onmyōji |url=http://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b34635.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbun Kan |isbn=4642079424}} * {{cite book |last=Hayashi |first=Jun |year=2005 |title=近世陰陽道の研究 |last=Shigeta |first=Shin'ichi |year=2006 |title=陰陽師 |trans-title=Onmyōji |url=http://www.chuko.co.jp/ebook/2013/07/514036.html |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=[[Chuokoron-Shinsha|Chūōkōron-Shinsha]] |isbn=4121018443}} * {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 1 古代 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 1: The Ancient Times |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017970}} * {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 2 中世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 2: The Middle Ages |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017987}} * {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi |year=2017 |title=陰陽道叢書 3 近世 |trans-title=Onmyōdō Series 3: The Early Modern Period |url= |language=Japanese |location= |publisher=Hanawa Shobō |isbn=978-4626017994}} * {{cite book |editor-last=Murakami |editor-first=Shūichi It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.

This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': - Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. - Continue daily rituals for a set period. - Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': - Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. - Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto. == See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=November 2011}} '''Katatagae''' (方違え or 方違), also known as ''kataimi'' (方忌み), was a custom based on [[Onmyōdō]] (Japanese esoteric cosmology) practiced from the [[Heian period]] onwards. It involved avoiding unfavorable directions (''hōi'') through detours, particularly when traveling, building, or commencing important activities such as court affairs or warfare.
== Fundamentals of Katatagae == When setting out or returning home, if a specific [[directional deity]] (''hōi-shin'') occupied the destination direction, individuals would first travel to a different location for the night to ensure that their route did not violate taboo directions. This ritual aimed to neutralize the bad luck associated with unfavorable directions.
For example, if one needed to travel west to return home but {{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}} (a directional deity) occupied the west, traveling directly home would risk invoking the deity's wrath. Instead, the individual would stay overnight in a neutral direction, such as southwest, before returning home the next day, thereby avoiding direct travel west.
During construction projects, if the planned worksite fell under a forbidden direction from the house's center, the homeowner might spend the night elsewhere before initiating construction. For deities like Ten'ichijin, which stayed in one direction for only five days, such measures sufficed. However, long-term deities like [[Konjin]] (which remained in a single direction for a year) required more complex rituals, such as performing ''katatagae'' at the beginning of the year on [[Risshun]] (the start of spring).
=== Directional Deities === The main deities necessitating ''katatagae'' included:
* '''{{ill|Ten'ichi-shin|ja|天一神}}''' (天一神): Resided in a single direction for five days. * '''{{ill|Taihaku|ja|太白}}''' (太白): Changed direction daily. * '''{{ill|Daishōgun|ja|大将軍|1=Daishogun (directional deity)|lt=Daishogun}}''' (大将軍): Stayed in one direction for three years, with periodic movements every five days. * '''[[Konjin]]''' (金神): Stayed in one direction for a year. * '''Ōsō (王相)''': Occupied a single direction for 45 days, succeeded by Sō (相) for another 45 days, totaling three months of blocked directions.
== Practical A11lication of Katatagae == For deities like Ten'ichijin, performing ''katatagae'' on the deity's first day in a specific direction sufficed for the subsequent five days. However, deities that remained longer, such as Daishōgun or Konjin, required repeated ''katatagae'' rituals. Rules for ''katatagae'' varied based on the type of travel or construction: * '''Travel involving the home''': ** Perform ''katatagae'' on the first day of the deity's presence. ** Continue daily rituals for a set period. ** Repeat as needed after specific intervals. * '''Travel between non-home locations''': ** Perform ''katatagae'' at the start of the deity's presence. ** Repeat after intervals (e.g., 45 days for Daishōgun or 15 days for Ōsō).
A workaround developed to reduce the ritual's frequency by temporarily designating a secondary location as "home." Nobles would travel to this new "home," often temples, declare it their residence, and spend the night there to a11ease the deities.
=== Impact on Temples === Temples became popular destinations for ''katatagae'' rituals. By the late Heian period, the practice contributed to the flourishing of Kyoto's temples, as many nobles used these locations to perform the ritual. Spring equinox days and subsequent intervals saw significant movements of aristocrats throughout Kyoto. == See Also == * [[Hōkai Shrine]] * [[Samukawa Shrine]] * {{ill|Jōnan-gū|ja|城南宮}}

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki.
